[[Corey Burton]] |music= |timeline=[[22 BBY]]–[[19 BBY]]''[[Star Wars: Timelines]]'' }} '''''Star Wars: The Clone Wars''''' is an [[wikipedia:Animated_series|animated television series]] set between the events of [[Star Wars: Episode II Attack of the Clones|''Star Wars:'' Episode II ''Attack of the Clones'']] and [[Star Wars: Episode III Revenge of the Sith|''Star Wars:'' Episode III ''Revenge of the Sith'']]. The series aired on [[Cartoon Network]] for five seasons from [[2008]] to [[2013]], while an abridged sixth season was released through [[Netflix]] in [[2014]]. The final, [[The Clone Wars: Season Seven|seventh season]] was released on [[Disney+]] in [[2020]]. ==Development== [[File:LWF.jpg|thumb|left|250px|(left to right) George Lucas, Dave Filoni, and Catherine Winder discuss the series.]] [[George Lucas]] first had the idea for a [[Clone Wars]] animated series in [[2002]].{{WebCite|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2008/06/29/movies/29itzk.html|text=Free to Follow His Heart Right Back to 'Star Wars'|author=Dave Itzkoff|date=2008-06-29|work=[[Wikipedia:The New York Times|''The New York Times'']]|archivedate=20181128222218}} ''[[Star Wars: Clone Wars]]'' aired on [[Cartoon Network]]{{SWArchive|url=clonewars/about/news/news20030220.html|text=Lucasfilm & Cartoon Network Announce: ''Star Wars: Clone Wars''}} from [[2003]] to [[2005]],{{SWArchive|url=clonewars/microseries/chapter/archive.html|text=''Clone Wars'' Chapter Archive}} produced by [[Genndy Tartakovsky]].{{SWArchive|url=clonewars/microseries/castcrew.html|text=''Clone Wars'' Cast & Crew}} Lucas then announced ''Star Wars: The Clone Wars'' at April 2005's [[Celebration III]], where he described ''Clone Wars'' as a "pilot television series" and said that ''The Clone Wars'' would "take that and turn it into a 3-D animated version full series."{{SWArchive|url=community/event/celebration/f20050419/indexp17.html|text=starwars.com at Celebration III - Thank the Maker: George Lucas}} [[Dave Filoni]] was hired as supervising director, and was familiar with previous ''Star Wars'' animation projects.{{WebCite|url=https://www.cbr.com/director-talks-star-wars-the-clone-wars|text=Director talks ''Star Wars: The Clone Wars''|author=Jami Philbrick|date=2008-10-07|work=[[Wikipedia:Comic Book Resources|''Comic Book Resources'']]|archivedate=20200112170945}}{{WebCite|url=http://www.scifi.com/scifiwire/index.php?category=0&id=60272|text=Q&A: ''Clone Wars'' TV Show Revealed|author=Ian Spelling|date=2008-09-19|work=[[Wikipedia:Syfy|''Sci Fi Wire'']]|archivedate=20081216075906}} Filoni's initial idea for the series revolved around a recurring cast of characters who traveled aboard a spaceship similar to the ''[[Millennium Falcon]]''; the ship's crew would have included a smuggler, his girlfriend, a [[Gungan]] named Lunker, a [[Jedi]] [[padawan]] named Ashla, and her [[Jedi Master]]. This early concept for ''The Clone Wars'' was designed not to interfere with existing continuity, and as such would have included only occasional appearances from film characters, such as [[Obi-Wan Kenobi]] and [[Anakin Skywalker]]. However, once Lucas became involved, he decided that the show would focus on the film characters, but would also include some of Filoni's ideas, such as Ashla (whose name was ultimately changed to [[Ahsoka Tano|Ahsoka]]).''[[The Art of Star Wars: The Clone Wars]]'' By [[February]] [[2006]], [[Lucasfilm Animation]] was working on animation tests and initial scripts, with Lucas playing a large part in the entire process.{{WebCite|url=http://www.movieweb.com/tv/news/90/11090.php|text=EXCLUSIVE INTERVIEW: Steve Sansweet Talks About the Upcoming ''Star Wars'' TV Shows!|author=Stephen Reedy|date=2006-02-15|work=[[Wikipedia:Valnet#MovieWeb|''MovieWeb'']]|archivedate=20080807173807}} The crew used [[Wikipedia:Autodesk|Autodesk]] [[Wikipedia:Software|software]] to animate both the film and the series. The [[Wikipedia:Maya (software)|Maya]] [[Wikipedia:3D modeler|3D modeling]] program was used to create highly detailed worlds, characters, and creatures.{{WebCite|url=http://usa.autodesk.com/adsk/servlet/item?siteID=123112&id=11774500&linkID=14271589|text=Autodesk Maya Software Serves as Animation Platform for New Star Wars: The Clone Wars Animated Feature Film and TV Series|date=2008-08-26|work=[[Wikipedia:Autodesk|Autodesk]] – Press Room Archive|archivedate=20171017002158|nolive=1}} Lucas and the animation team used this to create Zviz, a system that allowed the animation team complete freedom in framing shots.{{WebCite|url=https://www.awn.com/vfxworld/zviz-ilm-goes-interactive-previs|text=Zviz: ILM Goes Interactive with Previs|author=Barbara Robertson|date=2006-08-25|work=[[Wikipedia:Animation World Network|Animation World Network]]|archivedate=20200303013001}}{{WebCite|url=https://ew.com/tv/star-wars-the-clone-wars-dave-filoni-final-season-bad-batch-interview|text=''Clone Wars'' EP Dave Filoni breaks down the first episodes of the final season|author=Tim Leong|date=2020-03-02|work=''[[Wikipedia:Entertainment Weekly|Entertainment Weekly]]''|archivedate=20200706084837}} Script, character design, and art direction were completed at [[Industrial Light & Magic|ILM]], with the animation and rendering done at [[Lucasfilm Animation Singapore]], and also [[Polygon Pictures]] between 2011 and 2013.{{WebCite|url=https://www.ppi.co.jp/works/star-wars-the-clone-wars|text=Star Wars: The Clone Wars|work=[[Polygon Pictures]]'s official website|archivedate=20240614083143}} No rotoscoping was involved, however, ILM did use motion capture. The final assembly of each episode was done at [[Skywalker Ranch]].{{WebCite|url=http://theforce.net/swtv/story/3D_Star_Wars_Cartoon_News_103405.asp|text=3D ''Star Wars'' Cartoon News|author=Dustin Roberts|date=2007-02-07|work=[[TheForce.net]]|archivedate=20111129062941}} Lucas's involvement resulted in rewrites of most of the scripts, as Lucas insisted on more character development and that the show should not be based in one location, but as many as possible so as to make the story as interesting as possible to the viewer.{{SWArchive|url=theclonewars/blogs/f20070831/index.html|text=Lucas Gets Involved}} The animation style was inspired by 1960s-era puppets-in-space serials such as [[Wikipedia:Gerry Anderson|Gerry Anderson's]] [[Wikipedia:Thunderbirds (TV series)|''Thunderbirds'']],August 11-24 issue of ''TV Guide'', ''The Clones Strike Back'' as well as the character designs from the original ''Clone Wars'' series.{{SWArchive|url=theclonewars/blogs/f20071005/index.html|text=''Clone Wars'' Character Designer Kilian Plunkett}} Each episode of ''The Clone Wars'' was designed to be stand-alone, with an opening narration and moral to each story.{{SWArchive|url=theclonewars/blogs/f20070803/index.html|text=Introducing Catherine Winder}} In terms of story and cinematography, the series was directed as if it were a live-action series, and the episodes were produced in widescreen.{{WebCite|url=http://www.ew.com/ew/article/0,,20184842_2,00.html|text=George Lucas' 'Star Wars' plan|author=Joshua Rich|date=2008-03-18|work=''[[Wikipedia:Entertainment Weekly|Entertainment Weekly]]''|archivedate=20130922062435}} Writer [[Henry Gilroy]] gained inspiration for the episode titles from the pulpy adventure sci-fi serials of the 1930s.[[Star Wars Insider 103|''Star Wars Insider'' 103]] The series utilized unused ideas from Lucas' previous ''Star Wars'' projects. [[Paul Dini]] pitched at least two rejected ideas for episodes: One involved [[Jar Jar Binks]] trying to convince the [[Galactic Senate]] that there is life on an obscure planet, and the other involved [[Anakin Skywalker]] and [[Padmé Amidala]] hiring an [[Ewok]] assassin.{{WebCite|url=http://smodcast.com/episodes/the-dini-dossiers-pt-1|text=Fat Man on Batman #017: Paul Dini: The Dini Dossiers Pt. 1|author=Kevin Smith|date=2012-11-21|work=[[Wikipedia:SModcast|''SModcast'']]|archivedate=20121202170728}} In 2008, In an interview they gave to TheForce.net, Filoni and Gilroy stated that, while Lucas gave them creative freedom for most of the series, there were several characters and locations that were off-limits. In respect to the characters, Lucas asked not to use [[Han Solo]], [[Chewbacca]], [[Boba Fett]], or [[Jabba Desilijic Tiure|Jabba the Hutt]] when Gilroy was writing the show bible, but by the time of the next meeting, Lucas had changed his mind about the latter two characters. In respect to the locations, Lucas asked Gilroy and Filoni to not explore the planets seen in the films, with the exception of [[Coruscant]]. But as with the character restrictions, he eventually changed his mind.{{WebCite|url=http://theforce.net/jedicouncil/interview/henrygilroyanddavefiloni.asp|text=Interviews | Henry Gilroy and Dave Filoni|date=2008-12-29|work=[[TheForce.net]]|archivedate=20200608191740}} In the same interview, Filoni stated, “We could come up with ideas and present them to [Lucas] immediately, so there was no concern as to whether or not it “was” Star Wars. This series at least to George is NOT EU, it is a part of Star Wars as he sees it. I think if anything there was a period where Henry and I had to learn exactly what it took to be a part of George Lucas’ Star Wars, and tell the Star Wars story his way. We had to learn how to look at the Galaxy from his point of view and let go of some of what we considered canon after we found out the ideas were only EU. Really we had to “unlearn what we had learned” and go back to the movies as the defining source material.”  In the same year, in an interview featured in [[Star Wars Insider 103]], Gilroy explained, Lucas wanted “a process where he could be involved creatively at every step.” He and Filoni generated the initial 15 or so story ideas, developing them into premises, then met with Lucas for his input and approval, which was repeated with the final script for the story. As time went on, however, Lucas started to write his own story outlines for the writers to craft them into scripts. Gilroy said, “George is the Alpha and the Omega, and is involved in the story from beginning to end. On ''The Clone Wars'', that means from the initial story idea until the final sound mix” and that “just about every story now comes from ‘The Maker’ himself.”[[Star Wars Insider 103|''Star Wars Insider'' 103]] On [[March 11]], [[2013]], a video appeared on [[StarWars.com]], in which the crew explained that they were "winding down" the production of the series, and that they had finished the active production of new episodes.{{SW|url=news/a-new-direction-for-lucasfilm-animation|text=A New Direction for Lucasfilm Animation}} [[Lucasfilm Story Group]]'s [[Pablo Hidalgo]] stated that there were 13 story arcs left unfinished.{{Twitter|pablohidalgo|status/712339140000530432|[[Pablo Hidalgo]]|quote=There were 13 arcs left, all of which were previewed in some way at Celebration Europe or Celebration Anaheim or released online.|archivefile=File:TCW13ArcsLeft-HidalgoTwitter.png}} Writer [[Brent Friedman]] revealed on his Twitter account that scripts for the unproduced seasons 7 and 8 had been written prior to the series' cancelation.{{WebCite|url=http://www.theforce.net/story/front/The_Clone_Wars_Writer_Discusses_Unaired_Episodes_158107.asp|text=The Clone Wars Writer Discusses Unaired Episodes|date=2014-05-20|work=[[TheForce.net]]|archivedate=20200401065439}} [[James Arnold Taylor]], [[Obi-Wan Kenobi]]'s voice actor, has stated that prior to the series' cancelation, the actors had recorded at least two remaining unproduced seasons.{{OfficialYouTube|channel=@Nerdist|wplink=Nerdist|video=HPkj62C4F8s|text=How To Create Obi-Wan Kenobi's Voice (Talkin' Toons w/ Rob Paulsen)|quote=Star Wars Clone Wars' James Taylor explains how he created Obi-Wan Kenobi's voice with hints of Ewan McGregor in this clip from Talkin' Toons with Rob Paulsen!}} Since the end of the series, both Leland Chee and the official blog have posted chronological lists of episodes.{{Twitter|HolocronKeeper|status/443106707472801793|[[Leland Chee]]|date=2014-03-10|quote=216 116 T 301 303 101-115 117-121 201-203 217-219 204-214 220-222 305-307 302 304 308 122 309-311 215 312-322 S4 502-513 501 514-520 601-613|archivedate=20190722081618|nolive=1}}{{SW|url=news/star-wars-the-clone-wars-chronological-episodeorder|text=''Star Wars: The Clone Wars'' Chronological Episode Order}} ==Release and reception== [[File:SWTCW-poster.jpg|thumb|200px|The exclusive Celebration IV poster for the series]] The first [[:File:SWtimeline.jpg|image]] released from the series—that of a 3D animated [[Yoda]]—was shown at Toy Fair [[2007]].{{WebCite|url=http://www.jeditemplearchives.com/swexperience/toyfair2007/hasbro/pages/01intro-008.html|text=American International Toy Fair 2007|author=Chuck Paskovics, Peter Hauerstein|date=2007-02-10|work=[https://www.jeditemplearchives.com Jedi Temple Archives]|archivedate=20190509230437}} On [[May 27]], 2007, supervising director Dave Filoni and executive producer Catherine Winder gave a presentation at [[Celebration IV]] in which they debuted the first official trailer and poster, and discussed the production process of the previous two years.{{SWArchive|url=community/event/celebration/news20070517b.html|text=First Look: ''The Clone Wars'' Celebration IV Poster}} Filoni and Winder also held similar presentations at [[Celebration Europe (2007)|Celebration Europe]] and WonderCon [[2008]].{{SWArchive|url=community/event/celebration/news20070627.html|text=''The Clone Wars'' at Celebration Europe}}{{SWArchive|url=community/event/con/news20080221.html|text=''The Clone Wars'' News Saturday at WonderCon}} StarWars.com created a new division of the site dedicated to ''The Clone Wars'', which was regularly updated with relevant content, including news articles, behind the scenes videos, and blog entries by [[Pete Vilmur]].{{OfficialBlog|url=2007/07/27/starwarscom-launches-the-clone-wars-section|text=StarWars.com Launches ''The Clone Wars'' Section}} ===Television=== Lucas shopped ''The Clone Wars'' to various networks, but had difficulty securing a buyer due to the show's unique tone.{{WebCite|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/entertainment/7048440.stm|text=Lucas begins ''Star Wars'' TV series|date=2007-10-17|work=[[Wikipedia:BBC News|BBC News]]|archivedate=20200520060705}} Fox Broadcasting passed on it, and Cartoon Network was initially unsure. Several networks eventually got into a bidding war over the show.{{WebCite|url=http://blog.sfgate.com/tgoodman/2008/02/12/cartoon-network-lands-star-wars-the-clone-wars-tv-series-big-screen-movie-debuts-aug-15|text=Cartoon Network lands "Star Wars: The Clone Wars" TV series; big screen movie debuts Aug. 15.|author=Tim Goodman|date=2008-02-12|work=[[Wikipedia:SFGate|''SFGate'']]|archivedate=20171123044601}} [[Cartoon Network]] and [[Turner Network Television|TNT]] eventually won the broadcast rights, with [[Warner Bros.]] distributing a theatrical debut. Warner had tracked the series' development, and decided on a theatrical launch after viewing some footage.{{WebCite|url=http://variety.com/2008/digital/markets-festivals/animated-star-wars-to-hit-theaters-1117980757|text=Animated 'Star Wars' to hit theaters|author=Diane Garrett|date=2008-02-11|work=[[Wikipedia:Variety (magazine)|''Variety'']]|archivedate=20160123042209}} Stuart Snyder, former President of Cartoon Network, said he became interested in the new ''Clone Wars'' series immediately upon starting the job in May 2007. Snyder flew out to [[Wikipedia:San Francisco, California|San Francisco]], [[Wikipedia:California|California]] to screen several episodes, and told Lucas the only place he wanted to see the show was on Cartoon Network.{{WebCite|url=http://www.ajc.com/business/content/business/stories/2008/09/02/cartoon_clone_wars.html|text=Cartoon Network is eager to use the force|author=Kristi E. Swartz|date=2008-09-02|work=[[Wikipedia:The Atlanta Journal-Constitution|''The Atlanta Journal-Constitution'']]|archivedate=20080904044940}} It was announced on February 12, 2008 that the [[Star Wars: The Clone Wars (film)|feature film of the same name]] was to be released in theaters on August 15, 2008, with the TV debut in the fall.{{WebCite|url=http://www.usatoday.com/life/movies/news/2008-02-11-clone-wars-side_N.htm|text=New film, TV series fill in the 'Star Wars' blanks|author=Anthony Breznican|date=2008-02-11|work=[[Wikipedia:USA Today|''USA Today'']]|archivedate=20081101151222}} This announcement was accompanied by an update of the Hyperspace online fan club kit,{{Hyperspace|url=hyperspace/member/fankit|text=Hyperspace Fan Club Kit}} and an offering of [[Rex|Captain Rex]] and [[Ahsoka Tano]] T-shirts to Hyperspace members.{{SWArchive|url=collecting/shop/shopnews/news20080212c.html|text=Exclusive ''Clone Wars'' Prelaunch T-shirt}}{{SWArchive|url=collecting/shop/shopnews/news20080212b.html|text=Hyperspace Exclusive Ahsoka T-shirt}} The series premiered on October 3, 2008 at 9 p.m. on the Cartoon Network. It aired on Friday nights{{WebCite|url=http://enewsi.com/news.php?catid=194&itemid=13013|text=Moving Forward, The Force Is With Cartoon Network|author=Jay Cochran|date=2008-04-04|work=[https://enewsi.com Entertainment News International]|archivedate=20080404144039}} as part of an [[Wikipedia:Action-adventure|action-adventure]] block alongside [[Wikipedia:The Secret Saturdays|''The Secret Saturdays'']] and [[Wikipedia:Ben 10: Alien Force|''Ben 10: Alien Force'']], in an attempt to rejuvenate the network and compete with channels, such as [[Wikipedia:Nickelodeon (TV channel)|Nickelodeon]] and the [[Disney Channel]]. Snyder expressed confidence that the shows would help boost ratings: "You catch me at a time where I have a smile on my face because of our internal results," Snyder said. "I can say there's a little bit of bragging on the third quarter for us." In Canada, the first season aired on [[Wikipedia:CTV Television Network|CTV]]{{WebCite|url=http://www.ctv.ca/servlet/ArticleNews/story/CTVNews/20080602/ctv_fallseason_080602/20080602|text=CTV fall season puts focus on Canadian fare|author=[[Wikipedia:The Canadian Press|The Canadian Press]]|date=2008-06-02|work=[[Wikipedia:CTV Television Network|CTV Television Network]]|archivedate=20090227024203}} and [[Wikipedia:Space (TV channel)|SPACE]], while the second through fifth aired on [[Wikipedia:Teletoon (Canadian TV channel)|Teletoon]], which aired repeats of the first season as part of its Weekday Action Force block. In Australia, the first season aired on Channel Ten. On April 13, 2011, ABC3 aired repeats of first season and has since aired seasons two, three, and four. During May 4-5, ABC3 did a marathon of all Season 5 episodes, which was the first time Season 5 had aired on ABC3. The series was first aired in the UK on [[Wikipedia:Sky Movies|Sky Movies]] Premiere and Premiere HD every Saturday at 5.30PM with the first episode aired on October 25, 2008. As with all programs on Sky Movies, it ran for the first 22-minutes of its 30-minute slot with advertisements shown at the end so as not to interrupt the episodes.{{WebCite|url=http://movies.sky.com/movie/the-clone-wars|text=Star Wars: The Clone Wars (TV)|work=[[Wikipedia:Sky Movies|Sky Movies]]|archivedate=20100502111302|nolive=1}}{{WebCite|url=http://www.jedinews.co.uk/misc/articles/star-wars-the-clone-wars-to-debut-on-sky-movies-premier|text=''Star Wars: The Clone Wars'' to Debut on Sky Movies Premier|author=Adam Lamping|date=2008-09-26|work=[https://www.jedinews.com Jedi News]|archivedate=20181130122808}} Lucas expected ''The Clone Wars'' to run for four or five years, but planned to produce at least 100 episodes regardless of ratings.{{WebCite|url=http://www.comingsoon.net/movies/features/42983-exclusive-a-rare-sit-down-with-mr-george-lucas|text=Exclusive: A Rare Sit-Down with Mr. George Lucas|author=Edward Douglas|date=2008-03-17|work=[https://www.comingsoon.net ComingSoon.net]|archivedate=20180618184617}}{{SWArchive|url=theclonewars/news/news20070309.html|text=Lucas Talks: ''Star Wars'' on Television}}{{WebCite|url=http://uk.ign.com/articles/2007/03/06/paley-fest-george-lucas-gives-details-on-the-star-wars-tv-shows|text=Paley Fest: George Lucas Gives Details on the ''Star Wars'' TV Shows|author=Eric Goldman|date=2007-04-05|work=[[Wikipedia:IGN|''IGN'']]|archivedate=20190420181647}}{{WebCite|url=http://www.tvguide.com/news/lucas-wars-TV/070920-01|text=George Lucas, Episode II: ''Star Wars'' Strikes Back — on TV|author=Steve Sonsky|date=2007-09-20|work=[[Wikipedia:TV Guide|TV Guide]]|archivedate=20080921193236}} The first four seasons consisted of 22 episodes, the fifth of 20 episodes, and the sixth of 13 episodes. The [[Brothers (episode)|21st]] and [[Revenge (episode)|22nd]] episodes of [[The Clone Wars: Season Four|Season Four]] featured a red opening logo, as opposed to the series' standard yellow.{{SW|url=explore/the-clone-wars/ep421|text="Brothers" - ''The Clone Wars'' Episode Guide|nolive=1}} In the [[Secret Weapons|10th]], [[A Sunny Day in the Void|11th]], [[Missing in Action|12th]], and [[Point of No Return (The Clone Wars)|13th]] episodes of [[The Clone Wars: Season Five|Season Five]], the opening logo was changed to have a blue color in honor of [[R2-D2]]. TNT signed a multi-seasonal agreement to broadcast repeats of the series,{{WebCite|url=http://www.tvguide.com/news/080214-02|text=New Star Wars Series: Five Burning Questions Answered!|author=Sands, Rich|date=2008-02-14|work=[[Wikipedia:TV Guide|TV Guide]]|archivedate=20080825005520}}{{SWArchive|url=theclonewars/news20090129.html|text=''The Clone Wars'' Premieres on TNT in February}} though only aired a limited run before dropping the series.{{WebCite|url=http://theforce.net/latestnews/story/Is_The_Clone_Wars_Still_On_TNT_122070.asp|text=Is ''The Clone Wars'' Still on TNT?|author=Mike Barrick|date=2009-03-28|work=[[TheForce.net]]|archivedate=20190730163711}} Episodes began airing in syndication in fall 2012, in conjunction with the series' fifth season.{{SW|url=news/star-wars-the-clone-wars-season-5-and-nationwide-syndication|text=''Star Wars: The Clone Wars'' Season 5 and Nationwide Syndication|nolive=1}} ===Post-cancellation=== An abridged sixth season, dubbed [[The Clone Wars: The Lost Missions|The Lost Missions]], was released in the United States through [[Netflix]] and in Germany on [[Wikipedia:Super RTL|Super RTL]].{{WebCite|url=https://www.fernsehserien.de/star-wars-the-clone-wars/episodenguide/staffel-6/9660|text=Star Wars: The Clone Wars Staffel 6 Episodenguide|work=[[Wikipedia:Fernseh|Fernseh]]|archivedate=20190419012747}} (The Netflix episodes were part of a package deal that included the feature film and the first five seasons.) However, Cartoon Network sometimes used the sixth season episodes. Additional episodes were released in the form of [[The Clone Wars Legacy|''The Clone Wars'' Legacy]], a project that encompasses the comic book series [[Star Wars: Darth Maul — Son of Dathomir|''Star Wars: Darth Maul—Son of Dathomir'']], the novel ''[[Dark Disciple]]'', and 8 story reels that were posted to StarWars.com but were later taken off the site.{{SW|url=tv-shows/the-clone-wars-legacy|text=''The Clone Wars'' Legacy}} The series proved extremely popular with a loyal core of fans, who repeatedly petitioned for the revival of the series, often using the social media hashtag #SaveTheCloneWars. At the [[2018]] [[Wikipedia:San Diego Comic-Con|San Diego Comic-Con]], Dave Filoni led a panel celebrating the tenth anniversary of the series. The end of the panel saw the announcement that a [[The Clone Wars: Season Seven|final set of episodes]] would be released on the [[Disney+]] in [[2020]]. Filoni credited the immense fan support as being key in Disney's decision to revive the series. To commemorate the completion of the final season, the cast held a [[Wikipedia:D23 (Disney)|D23]] livestream on [[May 8]], [[2020]].{{D23|url=clonewars|text=Star Wars: The Clone Wars Cast Reunion Challenge}} ==Continuity== The series's creators explained that continuity with the [[Star Wars Legends|Expanded Universe]] was being taken very seriously, with Dave Filoni being well-versed in the material (although Lucas had the right to make slight adjustments for the sake of the story, and was consulted directly when dealing with the major characters). Lucasfilm told ''TV Guide'' that ''The Clone Wars'' would not supersede the original ''Clone Wars'' series in continuity, but would expand upon what the earlier series established. Dark Horse Comics editor [[Randy Stradley]] also claimed to have met with Lucasfilm Animation to discuss integrating the show with the [[Star Wars: Clone Wars (trade paperbacks)|''Clone Wars'' comics]].{{DHBoards|url=viewtopic.php?t=10498|thread=For Dark Horse Staff|nobackup=1}} However, the feature film and TV series introduced a number of continuity inconsistencies, many of which directly contradicted previously published sources and required [[Retroactive continuity|retcon]]s and major shifts in the previously established Clone Wars timeline which were never established by [[Leland Chee]].{{DHBoards|url=viewtopic.php?t=13886&postdays=0&postorder=asc&start=101|thread=''The Clone Wars'' continuity (Calling Mr. Chee)|nobackup=1}} There were several prominent examples of direct contradiction between ''The Clone Wars'' and previous ''Clone Wars'' media: *On [[August 8]], 2009, ''[[Republic Commando]]'' author [[Karen Traviss]] stated that she was leaving the ''Star Wars'' franchise due to alleged continuity issues raised with the publication of ''[[The Art of Star Wars: The Clone Wars]]'',{{WebCite|url=http://karentraviss.typepad.com/blog/2009/08/end-of-one-era-start-of-another.html|text=End of one era, start of another…|author=Karen Traviss|date=2009-08-08|work=Karen Traviss's Blog|archivedate=20090810234747}} which included details regarding the [[Mandalorian/Legends|Mandalorians]] that clashed with the continuity set-up by her novels; this was most likely in reference to the trilogy of episodes involving [[Mandalore]] in the show’s second season ("[[The Mandalore Plot]]," "[[Voyage of Temptation]]" and "[[Duchess of Mandalore]]") in which the main Mandalorian faction is portrayed as a pacifist society living on Mandalore, which itself is portrayed as a radioactive desert, contradicting all previous descriptions.{{TCW|The Mandalore Plot}} Prior to the April 25, 2014, reorganization of canon, Leland Chee{{Twitter|HolocronKeeper|status/3221250171|[[Leland Chee]]|nobackup=1}} and [[Sue Rostoni]] maintained that the complications would eventually be worked-out.{{SWBoards|url=thread.jspa?threadID=270262&start=165#17701245|thread=Fan reactions- The Latest in Breaking News reactions- SPOILER WARNING!!!!!!|user=[[Sue Rostoni]]|date=2009-08-11|time=11:04 AM|quote=As for the rest, try not to get too caught up in it -- I don't think it's as huge an issue as is being played out. Not to belittle continuity at all -- that's not what I'm saying -- just have faith in Leland, et al, to work this out. We've always hinted that there will be continuity issues and that we'd have to wait until further along into the animated series to resolve them all, but it will work out.}} The two portrayals of the planet were reconciled with retcons issued by ''[[The Essential Atlas]]'' and the subsequent ''[[Star Wars: The Clone Wars: New Battlefronts: The Visual Guide]]'', which states that (much like Earth) the vast, white-sand deserts seen in the aforementioned episodes was but one region of the larger, varied landscape of Mandalore's surface. *The use of [[Eta-2 Actis-class light interceptor|Eta-2 ''Actis''-class interceptors]] at the same point in the timeline Anakin is knighted,{{CW|21}} whereas in ''The Clone Wars'' [[Delta-7B Aethersprite-class light interceptor|Delta-7B ''Aethersprite''-class light interceptors]] are used, which were ostensibly used before the later Eta-2s. In addition, some elements from works like the [[Star Wars: Revenge of the Sith (novelization)|novelization of ''Revenge of the Sith'']] are being retconned. For example, in the novelization, Anakin remarks that he has only read about [[Grievous]]' [[IG-100 MagnaGuard]]s in an intel report, despite having fought them in the show.{{TCW|Duel of the Droids}} Another very noticeable continuity error is the fact that Anakin has his scar, which he received shortly after the [[Battle of Rendili]] from [[Asajj Ventress]]. He then injured Ventress so severely that she was forced to recuperate until the [[Battle of Boz Pity (Clone Wars)|Battle of Boz Pity]], after which she deserted the CIS and the war for good. However, he was originally shown to receive his scar in [[20 BBY/Legends|20 BBY]], while the series begins in [[22 BBY/Legends|22 BBY]]. *In 2003, the reference book ''[[Inside the Worlds of Star Wars: Attack of the Clones]]'' established that Jedi Master [[Eeth Koth]] died during the [[First Battle of Geonosis]] when enemy fire brought down his gunship. However, the character was "resurrected" by the project; he was first mentioned as fighting in the Clone Wars in the 2008 novel ''[[The Clone Wars: Wild Space]]'' and was later featured prominently in the second-season episode "[[Grievous Intrigue]]." *The third season saw an evolution in the appearance of the main and supporting characters along wit. Ahsoka Tano's new attire represented a more mature look. Anakin Skywalker and Obi-Wan Kenobi's attire was adjusted to be closer to how they appeared in [[Star Wars: Episode III Revenge of the Sith|''Revenge of the Sith'']].{{SWArchive|url=theclonewars/newcharacterlooks/index.html|text=A New Look for ''The Clone Wars'' Heroes}} *In the third season episode "[[Citadel Rescue]]," Jedi Master [[Even Piell]] died during a [[Battle of Lola Sayu|mission]] around the second year of the war. In the 2008 novel ''[[Coruscant Nights I: Jedi Twilight]]'', written by [[Michael Reaves]], [[Even Piell/Legends|Even Piell]] lived through the Clone Wars and died during the [[Great Jedi Purge/Legends|Great Jedi Purge]] in 19 BBY on Coruscant. *StarWars.com confirmed that Darth Maul survived the [[Battle of Naboo]] and would appear in [[The Clone Wars: Season Four|Season Four]] of the series.{{SWArchive|url=news/darth_maul_returns_2012.html|text=Darth Maul Returns to the Star Wars Saga This Spring!}} Maul returned in the [[Brothers (episode)|21st episode]], living on [[Lotho Minor]]. *The fifth-season opener "[[Revival (episode)|Revival]]" featured the death of Jedi Master [[Adi Gallia]] by Savage Opress [[Skirmish on Florrum|on]] [[Florrum]], despite having previously been slain by Grievous during the [[Battle of Boz Pity (Clone Wars)|Battle of Boz Pity]], one of the final battles of the Clone Wars in the 2005 comic [[Obsession 5|''Obsession'' 5]]. *The fifth season finale "[[The Wrong Jedi]]" presented that [[Barriss Offee]] fell to the [[dark side of the Force]] and [[Bombing of the Jedi Temple hangar|destroys]] the [[Jedi Temple/Legends|Jedi Temple]] hangar, framing Ahsoka Tano, and [[Trial of Ahsoka Tano|initiating]] an investigation against Tano, but Anakin ultimately discovers her plot and Offee gets arrested and taken away to prison. However, the daily webstrips series ''[[Reversal of Fortune]]'' and the [[Databank (original)|Databank]] entry of [[Felucia]] originally stated that [[Barriss Offee/Legends|Barriss Offee]] was killed at the end of the Clone Wars during [[Order 66/Legends|Order 66]] on [[Felucia/Legends|Felucia]], reflecting the original version of Order 66 in [[Star Wars: Episode III Revenge of the Sith|''Star Wars'': Episode III ''Revenge of the Sith'']], in which Offee was to die along [[Luminara Unduli]] and [[Shaak Ti]], whose death scenes were too omitted.{{WebCite|url=http://www.ccbanana.com/main/interviews/starwars1.htm|text=C.C. Banana Interviews the Stars of ''STAR WARS''!|archivedate=20070512233219}} As Offee was depicted as still serving the [[Jedi Order/Legends|Jedi Order]], it has been assumed in the Expanded Universe continuity that Offee remained at the Order through unknown reasons. Furthermore, Offee was intended to originally kill herself at the end of the episode, which would have contradicted her post-''The Clone Wars'' EU appearances definitively, but Dave Filoni cut the scene because he "had plans" for the character, which ultimately never came to fruition by the show's cancelation.{{SW|url=news/swca-the-untold-clone-wars-panel-liveblog|text=SWCA: The Untold ''Clone Wars'' Panel Liveblog}} *If the show had not been canceled, there was going to be an episode arc in the seventh season focusing on [[Maul]], which would have delved into his origins. While the episodes went unmade, their scripts were adapted into [[Dark Horse Comics]]' comic book series [[Star Wars: Darth Maul — Son of Dathomir|''Star Wars: Darth Maul—Son of Dathomir'']], which revealed [[Mother (title)|Mother]] [[Talzin]] to be Maul's mother, as well [[Savage Opress]]'. However, the novel ''[[Darth Plagueis]]'' had previously established that a [[Human/Legends|Human]] [[Nightsisters/Legends|Nightsister]] named [[Kycina]] was [[Darth Maul/Legends|Maul's]] mother, and the short story ''[[Restraint]]'' identified her as [[Savage Opress/Legends|Opress]]' mother as well. *One of the unproduced episodes due the show's cancelation would have featured the [[Yuuzhan Vong]], the extra-galactic species which first appeared in [[Del Rey]]'s [[1999]]-[[2003]] book series ''[[Star Wars: The New Jedi Order]]''. According to [[Pablo Hidalgo]], the episode wouldn't have specified the Vong's origins but only imply that they came from [[Yuuzhan Vong galaxy|another galaxy]], just as ''[[The New Jedi Order: Vector Prime]]'' originally established.{{Twitter|pablohidalgo|status/755516241083768833|[[Pablo Hidalgo]]|quote=There were from 'elsewhere.' So, probably. Story never got into it.|archivefile=File:TCWYuuzhanVongOrigin-HidalgoTwitter.png}} However, Hidalgo has stated that per George Lucas' vision of the Force, the Yuuzhan Vong weren't immune to the Force in the story.{{Twitter|pablohidalgo|status/755405799527243777|[[Pablo Hidalgo]]|quote=@gudenruben @BFett_ @ZRIKEitter They weren't Force-immune in that story.|archivefile=File:YuuzhanVongCloneWarsForceImmune-HidalgoTwitter.png}} This would have contradicted the EU, as the Yuuzhan Vong were established as immune to the Force (except to the [[Force lightning/Legends|Force lightning]] and [[Force net]] attacks) in ''[[The New Jedi Order: Edge of Victory I: Conquest]]''. *Likewise, in a similar way to Barriss Offee and the Yuuzhan Vong, [[Jaime King]]'s husband, Kyle Newman has stated that there were several unfinished episodes planned for the sixth season of the show featuring [[Aurra Sing]], including one in which Sing was to be killed.{{YouTube|video=DU6WwLoQaqs|text=Solo Live Reaction|channel=channel/UCxnEDVUZe8-j61nKo4HzOYg|name=Steele Wars|quote=You know, she actually died befo... There were episodes of The Clone Wars season 6 that I was in, which were deleted and she was in that too and she died.}} Had this been canonized, it would have contradicted several of [[Aurra Sing/Legends|Aurra Sing's]] appearances in EU stories set during the [[Galactic Civil War/Legends|Galactic Civil War]] and the [[New Republic era|New Republic]] and [[New Jedi Order era]]s, precisely ''[[Coruscant Nights II: Street of Shadows]]'', ''[[Star Wars: Demolition]]'', and ''[[Legacy of the Force: Tempest]]''. Eventually, in the new Canon continuity Sing's fate was addressed in the [[Star Wars Anthology Series|''Star Wars'' Anthology]] film, ''[[Solo: A Star Wars Story]]'', in which it was established that [[Tobias Beckett]] pushed Sing to her death. Initially supervising director Dave Filoni defended any differences in continuity: "But there's never an implicit connection between the micro-series that Cartoon Network did previously and the series that we're doing now. I personally as a fan never think of it as discrediting any of the other material, it's just that other material is from a different point of view, a different look at the war and take on the war. It's an ever-Expanding Universe in a lot of ways."{{WebCite|url=https://www.cbr.com/director-talks-star-wars-the-clone-wars|text=Director talks ''Star Wars: The Clone Wars''|author=Philbrick, Jami|date=2008-10-07|archivedate=20200112170945}} At his Celebration IV presentations, Filoni showed a series of slides showing several character sculpture designs for the series—stating that they would share some of the stylization of the original ''Clone Wars'' series, but would also have some of the realistic look of the films. He explained, however, that elements of the series would differ slightly from what has been previously established in continuity.{{SWArchive|url=theclonewars/blogs/news20070727b.html|text=''The Clone Wars'' Celebration IV Panel -- Part 2}} Changes were made from the original ''Clone Wars'' series: Obi-Wan doesn't wear as much of his clone general armor, Grievous has a cough prior to the [[Battle of Coruscant]], and the Jedi fighting styles are more like the films, and not like some of the exaggerated Jedi abilities portrayed in the original series. Eventually Filoni would go on record to state that the Expanded Universe and ''The Clone Wars'' television series "don't live in the same universe".{{InsiderCite|134|Behind and Beyond the Battle Lines}} ''The Clone Wars'' was classified as [[Canon|T-canon]] in the [[Holocron continuity database]].{{Twitter|HolocronKeeper|status/402147701795463168|[[Leland Chee]]|archivedate=20190722105356}}{{SWBoards|url=thread.jspa?threadID=152583&start=01755#17147721|thread=Holocron continuity database questions|user=[[Leland Chee|Tasty Taste]]|date=2008-02-20|time=4:19 PM}} However, on [[April 25]], 2014, it was announced that the Expanded Universe was to be re-branded as ''Star Wars'' Legends and that the only past material that would be considered canon would be the [[original trilogy]] and [[prequel trilogy]] films and ''The Clone Wars,'' while also enshrining Episodes 1-6 and The Clone Wars as the immovable truth for both timelines, thus negating any concerns regarding conflicts between the series and past ''Star Wars'' Legends material for content that was produced in the Canon timeline. Novelizations based on the series, however, are not considered canon, nor are video game releases or comics, with the exception of Legacy material and any other upcoming projects based on material that was developed for the television series that ultimately did not make it to broadcast.{{SW|url=news/the-legendary-star-wars-expanded-universe-turns-a-new-page|text=The Legendary Star Wars Expanded Universe Turns a New Page}}{{SW|url=news/sdcc-2014-star-wars-a-new-dawn-panel-liveblog|text=SDCC 2014: ''Star Wars: A New Dawn'' Panel – Liveblog}} Reference works based on the series, such as ''[[Star Wars: The Clone Wars Character Encyclopedia]]'', are considered part of the "Legends" continuity and are not considered canon sources, albeit a canonical [[Star Wars: The Clone Wars: Character Encyclopedia - Join the Battle!|reference titles]] now exist for the series' characters. As a consequence of the 2014 reboot, the initial multimedia project for the series was rendered non-canonical to the current continuity. ==Adaptations and tie-in media== [[Sue Rostoni]] first stated in June 2007 that a publishing program based on the series was possible, but had not yet been discussed.{{SWBoards|url=thread.jspa?threadID=222689&start=5040#16718416|thread=Books, Comics, & Television VIPs|user=[[Sue Rostoni]]|date=2007-06-19|time=9:28 PM|quote=We're not quite there yet. As far as I know, there haven't been any discussions around a publishing program just yet.}} [[Rob Coleman]] reported that licensees had a very positive reaction upon first seeing footage from the series.{{Hyperspace|url=hyperspace/member/audiocast/audio/SW_dvdpressevent_03_2005-10-10.mp3|series=AudioCast|text=Frank Oz and Rob Coleman Q&A: DVD Press Event}} A [[Dark Horse Comics]] volume was made for [[Wikipedia:Free Comic Book Day|Free Comic Book Day]].{{DarkHorse|url=Comics/16-214/Free-Comic-Book-Day-2009-Star-Wars-The-Clone-Wars-DH-All-Ages-Split-Issue|text=Free Comic Book Day 2009 ''Star Wars: The Clone Wars''/DH All Ages Split Issue}} To accompany the [[The Clone Wars: Season One|Season One]] episodes, StarWars.com hosted a [[Star Wars: The Clone Wars (webcomics)|weekly web comic]], as well as a [[HoloNet News (original website)|HoloNet News]] podcast. [[David Tennant]]'s performance of Professor [[Huyang]] in the fifth season episodes "[[A Test of Strength]]" and "[[A Necessary Bond]]" won him the Outstanding Performer in an Animated Program, while the series itself garnered the Outstanding Special Class Animated Program award.{{SW|url=news/star-wars-the-clone-wars-wins-two-daytime-emmy-awards|text=''Star Wars: The Clone Wars'' Wins Two Daytime Emmy Awards}} In 2014, the series was also nominated for four daytime Emmys,{{WebCite|url=http://www.ew.com/article/2014/05/01/star-wars-clone-wars-emmy-noms|text='Star Wars: The Clone Wars' gets 4 daytime Emmy noms|author=James Hibberd|date=2014-05-01|archivedate=20160601121809}}{{WebCite|url=http://emmyonline.com/day_41st_nominations|text=THE NATIONAL ACADEMY OF TELEVISION ARTS & SCIENCES ANNOUNCES THE 41st ANNUAL DAYTIME EMMY® AWARD NOMINATIONS|date=2014-05-01|work=emmyonline.com|archivedate=20191109084036}} winning two.{{WebCite|url=http://www.theforce.net/story/front/The_Clone_Wars_Wins_Two_Daytime_Emmy_Awards_158560.asp|text=The Clone Wars Wins Two Daytime Emmy Awards|author=Eric|date=2014-06-21|work=TheForce.net|archivedate=20160412103641}} In 2021, the series' revived seventh season was nominated for three Emmys.{{SW|url=news/star-wars-the-clone-wars-final-season-emmy-awards|text=''Star Wars: The Clone Wars'' Nominated for 3 Emmy Awards}} ==Media== {{Mediacat|from=1|imagecat=Images from Star Wars: The Clone Wars (TV series)}} ===Seasons=== {{SeeAlso|The Clone Wars Legacy}} {|class="wikitable" !
